difficulty_factors: Iron oxide pigments bond electrostatically — chemical dissolution alone insufficient
Silk limits available agents — no enzymes, no bleach, no heat, no alkali

diy.title: Foundation Makeup on Silk
mixSteps: Pour a small amount of undiluted Dawn dish soap onto the stain
If making a spray, mix 1 tbsp Dawn with 2 cups warm water in a spray bottle

steps.instruction: Sprinkle baking soda or cornstarch on the stain. Wait 5 minutes.
steps.why: The powder soaks up surface oil before it sinks deeper into the fabric.

steps.instruction: Brush off the powder with a clean white cloth.
steps.why: Brushing removes the oil the powder absorbed.

steps.instruction: Put a drop of dish soap on the stain. Blot gently it in.
steps.why: Dish soap is made to cut through grease and oil.

steps.instruction: Let it sit for 2-3 minutes. Rinse gently with cold water.
steps.why: Cool water helps the soap carry the oil away from the fabric.

steps.instruction: Repeat if needed. Lay flat on a towel to air dry — no heat, no wringing.
steps.why: Heat and agitation damage delicate fibers. Lay flat to keep the shape.

diy.ifStillThere: If the stain persists after soap, try rubbing alcohol on a cotton swab and dab the stain. For motor oil and automotive grease on car upholstery, an automotive all-purpose cleaner (APC) works better than household products.
proTips: Never wring or twist delicate fabrics — gently press out moisture between towels.
When in doubt, take it to a professional cleaner who specializes in delicates.
Never add water to an oil stain first — water and oil don't mix, and you'll spread it.
diy.safety: Test on an inside seam or hidden area first — delicate fabrics can react unpredictably. Never use bleach, pH-neutral delicate wash, or hot water. Use cold water only. Handle gently — rubbing or wringing damages the fibers. Lay flat to dry.
diy.whyThisWorks: Foundation — cosmetics contain oils, waxes, and pigments that bond to surfaces — the oil spreads the color deeper. Silk absorbs foundation oils into its delicate fibers. The oil bonds with the natural oils already in the fiber, and harsh solvents risk damaging the material. Cold water and gentle cleaning agents are the only safe approach — anything stronger risks destroying the fiber along with the stain.

home.title: Foundation on Silk — Confident Removal
home.confidenceNote: Success depends on surface type (Silk), delicate material. Fresh stains respond better than set stains.

primaryProduct.name: K2r Spot-Lifter
primaryProduct.why: Dry cleaning solvent safely dissolves oil and grease without water — ideal for delicate fabrics that can't handle wet treatment

alternativeProducts.name: The Laundress Delicate Wash
alternativeProducts.why: Enzyme-free formula with surfactants that lift oil gently

alternativeProducts.name: Cornstarch (pre-treatment)
alternativeProducts.why: Absorbs oil from fabric before washing — zero chemical risk to delicate fibers

home.prepStep: Blot excess wet stain with paper towels—do NOT rub. Avoid heat (dryer/iron) until fully treated, as heat sets petroleum stains permanently.

method.instruction: Blot excess wet stain with paper towels—do NOT rub. Avoid heat (dryer/iron) until fully treated, as heat sets petroleum stains permanently. Work quickly — the sooner you start, the better the result.
method.why: Removes bulk material so the product can focus on the remaining stain.

method.instruction: Apply Dawn Platinum Dish Soap directly to the stain. Work it in with a soft brush or cloth in gentle circular motions.
method.why: Allows the Dawn Platinum Dish Soap to penetrate and break the stain's bond to the fiber.

method.instruction: Gently dab the area with a soft brush or cloth to work the product into the stain.
method.why: Physical agitation plus chemical action loosens the stain's bond to fibers.

method.instruction: Rinse with cold water, rubbing gently to remove all product residue.
method.why: Cool water helps remove any remaining stain residue and flush it away.

method.instruction: wash in cold water with appropriate detergent for this fabric type and inspect before drying. Inspect the stain before drying — do not use a dryer until it is completely gone.
method.why: Final wash removes product residue. Air drying prevents heat-setting any remaining stain.

materialWarnings.warning: NEVER use enzyme-based cleaners (OxiClean, Zout, Biz) — enzymes digest protein fibers, permanently destroying the fabric
Use cold water only — heat sets protein stains and can damage delicate fibers
Handle gently — never wring, twist, or scrub aggressively
Heat permanently sets stains. Do not use dryer, iron, or hot water until stain is completely gone.

ifItDoesntWork.nextProduct: Professional dry cleaner — oil on delicate fibers often needs solvent-based treatment
ifItDoesntWork.escalation: If the stain persists after Professional dry cleaner — oil on delicate fibers often needs solvent-based treatment, take it to a professional cleaner who specializes in silk. Explain what products you already tried so they can adjust their approach.

proTips: Fresh foundation stains respond much faster than set stains.
Always test any product on a hidden area first—fabric dyes and finishes vary.
Never use the dryer or heat until the stain is completely gone; heat can lock it in permanently.
home.whyThisWorks: Oil stains on protein fibers respond best to dry methods or gentle surfactants. Cornstarch absorbs surface oil, and dry cleaning solvents dissolve the rest without water damage. Enzyme-based degreasers would damage silk and wool fibers.
home.timeEstimate: 15–30 minutes (may require 2–3 repeat cycles for older stains)

pro.title: Foundation — Oil/Pigment Removal Protocol (Silk)
pro.stainChemistry: Oil and grease stains (cooking oil, motor oil, cosmetics, sunscreen) contain lipid molecules that repel water. Fresh stains sit on the fiber surface; aged stains penetrate and can degrade the fiber's polymers. POG (Paint Oil Grease remover) is a volatile dry solvent with strong affinity for lipids—it dissolves the oil droplets and lifts them away. VDS (Volatile Dry Solvent) provides a gentler alternative. Steam and heat activate the solvent's vapor pressure, improving penetration. Post-treatment with NSD (neutral synthetic detergent) removes residual solvent and oil micro-droplets.

spottingProtocol.technique: Solvent extraction — dissolves and lifts oil from the fiber
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply POG directly to the oil stain. POG is a volatile dry solvent with strong affinity for lipids — it dissolves oil droplets on contact. Tamp gently and blot with absorbent cloth. For delicate fabrics, use VDS (Volatile Dry Solvent) instead.

spottingProtocol.technique: Secondary solvent — targets stubborn or polymerized oils
spottingProtocol.instruction: If POG plateaus, apply amyl acetate for stronger solvent action. Amyl acetate is especially effective on older or polymerized oil stains. Blot with clean absorbent cloth between applications. Never use on acetate fabrics.

spottingProtocol.technique: Detergent flush — emulsifies remaining micro-droplets of oil
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply NSD to emulsify any remaining oil micro-droplets and remove solvent residue. The detergent surrounds oil particles and allows them to be flushed away. Rinse thoroughly with cool water.
Apply diluted acetic acid to restore fiber to its natural state. Blot dry with clean cloth and allow to air dry.

pro.escalation: If VDS/POG reaches saturation, the oil may have polymerized or oxidized into the fiber polymer. Try sequential treating: first VDS to lift surface oil, then low-pH detergent to emulsify residual oil particles. For automotive greases (polyunsaturated), amyl acetate provides stronger solvent action than VDS.
pro.customerExplanation: This was a tricky one — makeup on your garment is one of the more challenging stains we deal with. Makeup combines oil and pigment — the oil spreads the color deeper into the material. Results depend on how long the stain had been set before treatment — some improvement may continue over the next few days.
commonMistakes: Using water before solvent—water causes the oil to emulsify and spread into the fiber, making it harder to remove.
Over-saturation with solvent—excess solvent spreads the stain and leaves a residue ring.
Not using proper ventilation—solvents produce fumes that require extraction.
Applying heat without solvent—heat can polymerize the oil and make removal harder.
pro.neutralization: After solvent extraction, post-treat with NSD to remove any residual oil particles and solvent vapor. Use mild alkali (pH 8-9) to ensure complete emulsification. Final rinse with cool water and mild acidic rinse (pH 4-5) to restore fiber pH and close the cuticle.
pro.fabricRisks: Acetate and triacetate cannot tolerate solvents—use NSD only. Delicate wools should use gentle VDS instead of harsh POG. Synthetics (polyester, acrylic) tolerate solvent chemistry well.

professional.name: Pyratex® POG
professional.amazonSearchQuery: Pyratex POG paint oil grease remover
professional.note: Dissolves the oil/wax carrier phase of cosmetics. First-line professional treatment. Use on dry fabric.

professional.name: Amyl Acetate (Banana Oil)
professional.note: Stronger solvent for long-wear and waterproof cosmetic film-formers. Dissolves polymer coatings that protect pigment.

professional.name: NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent)
professional.note: Suspends and flushes pigment particles after solvent dissolves the carrier. Essential for complete cosmetic stain removal.

professional.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 6% (Color-Safe)
professional.note: Bleaches persistent cosmetic dye staining. Apply after solvent and surfactant steps. Monitor colorfastness carefully.

household.name: Rubbing Alcohol (70% Isopropyl)
household.note: Dissolves cosmetic oil carriers and many film-formers. Apply via cloth. First-line home treatment for makeup stains.

household.name: Bioderma Sensibio Micellar Water
household.note: Designed to remove makeup — works on fabric too. Micelles capture oil and pigment simultaneously. Gentle option.

household.name: Dawn Ultra Dish Soap (Blue)
household.note: Excellent grease-cutter for cosmetic oil phase. Apply directly to stain, work in, let sit 5 minutes.

household.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 3%
household.note: For persistent cosmetic dye ghosts. Apply to discolored area after oil/wax removal. Test colorfastness first.

household.name: Carbona Stain Devils #5 (Fat & Cooking Oil)
household.amazonSearchQuery: Carbona Stain Devils fat cooking oil
household.note: Consumer-grade solvent effective on the oil/wax base of cosmetics. More aggressive than dish soap. Follow label directions.

If making a rocíe, mezcle 1 tbsp Dawn with 2 cups agua tibia in a rocíe bottle

steps.instruction: Cepille off the powder with a un paño blanco limpio.

steps.instruction: Put a drop of jabón para platos on the mancha. Seque a toques gently it in.

steps.instruction: Repita if needed. Lay flat on a towel to seque al aire — no heat, no wringing.

diy_es.ifStillThere: If the mancha persists after soap, try alcohol isopropílico on a algodón swab and dab the mancha. For aceite de motor and automotive grasa on tapicería del auto, an automotive all-purpose cleaner (APC) works better than household products.
proTips: Nunca wring or twist delicate fabrics — gently press out moisture between towels.
When in doubt, take it to a professional cleaner who specializes in delicates.
Nunca add water to an aceite mancha first — water and aceite don't mezcle, and you'll spread it.

diy_es.whyThisWorks: base de maquillaje — cosmetics contain oils, waxes, and pigments that bond to surfaces — the aceite spreads the color deeper. seda absorbs base de maquillaje oils into its delicate fibers. The aceite bonds with the natural oils already in the fiber, and harsh solvents risk damaging the material. Cold water and gentle cleaning agents are the only safe approach — anything stronger risks destroying the fiber along with the mancha.

pro_es.title: Protocolo Profesional: Base De Maquillaje en Seda
pro_es.stainChemistry: aceite and grasa stains (cooking aceite, aceite de motor, cosmetics, protector solar) contain lipid molecules that repel water. Fresh stains sit on the fiber surface; aged stains penetrate and can degrade the fiber's polymers. POG (pintura aceite grasa retirer) is a volatile seque solvent with strong affinity for lipids—it dissolves the aceite droplets and lifts them away. VDS (Volatile Seque Solvent) provides a gentler alternative. Steam and heat activate the solvent's vapor pressure, improving penetration. Post-treatment with NSD (neutral synthetic detergent) retires residual solvent and aceite micro-droplets.

spottingProtocol.technique: Solvent extraction — dissolves and lifts oil from the fiber
spottingProtocol.instruction: Aplique POG directly to the aceite mancha. POG is a volatile seque solvent with strong affinity for lipids — it dissolves aceite droplets on contact. Tamp gently and seque a toques with absorbent cloth. For delicate fabrics, use VDS (Volatile Seque Solvent) instead.

spottingProtocol.technique: Secondary solvent — targets stubborn or polymerized oils
spottingProtocol.instruction: If POG plateaus, aplique amyl acetate for stronger solvent action. Amyl acetate is especially effective on older or polymerized aceite stains. Seque a toques with clean absorbent cloth between applications. Nunca use on acetate fabrics.

spottingProtocol.technique: Detergent flush — emulsifies remaining micro-droplets of oil
spottingProtocol.instruction: Aplique NSD to emulsify any remaining aceite micro-droplets and retire solvent residue. The detergent surrounds aceite particles and allows them to be flushed away. Enjuague thoroughly with cool water.
Aplique diluted acetic acid to restore fiber to its natural state. Seque a toques seque with clean cloth and allow to seque al aire.

pro_es.escalation: If VDS/POG reaches saturation, the aceite may have polymerized or oxidized into the fiber polymer. Try sequential treating: first VDS to lift surface aceite, then low-pH detergent to emulsify residual aceite particles. For automotive greases (polyunsaturated), amyl acetate provides stronger solvent action than VDS.
pro_es.customerExplanation: Esta fue una mancha difícil — base de maquillaje en su prenda es una de las manchas más complicadas que tratamos. Las manchas de aceite repelen el agua, por lo que el lavado regular no las elimina. Los resultados dependen de cuánto tiempo estuvo la mancha antes del tratamiento — puede seguir mejorando en los próximos días.
commonMistakes: Using water before solvent—water causes the aceite to emulsify and spread into the fiber, making it harder to retire.
Over-saturation with solvent—excess solvent spreads the mancha and leaves a residue ring.
Not using proper ventilation—solvents produce fumes that require extraction.
Apliqueing heat without solvent—heat can polymerize the aceite and make removal harder.

pro_es.fabricRisks: Acetate and triacetate cannot tolerate solvents—use NSD only. Delicate wools should use gentle VDS instead of harsh POG. Synthetics (poliéster, acrylic) tolerate solvent chemistry well.

title: Foundation on Silk
stainChemistry: Foundation makeup is an oil-in-water or water-in-oil emulsion containing: (1) pigments (iron oxides for color, titanium dioxide for opacity, zinc oxide for sun protection); (2) oils (dimethicone, cyclopentasiloxane, mineral oil) for skin-feel; (3) film-formers (acrylates, PVP/VA) for wear time; (4) emulsifiers to hold it together. On silk (fibroin protein fiber), oil penetrates the loose amorphous regions between crystalline beta-sheet structures. Silk's smooth surface actually resists oil absorption initially, but once penetrated, the oil bonds to non-polar amino acid residues (glycine, alanine) along the peptide backbone. The iron oxide pigments are the primary staining agents — they're inorganic particles that bond electrostatically to fiber surfaces and resist most solvents.
whyThisWorks: Foundation requires an oil-dissolving step first — break the emulsion and dissolve the oil/silicone carrier. This releases the pigment particles from the film-former matrix. Then surfactant agitation physically dislodges the pigment particles and suspends them for rinsing. The iron oxide pigments are chemically stable, so we're relying on physical removal (loosening and flushing) rather than chemical dissolution. Silk is pH-sensitive (4.0-8.0) and dissolves in strong alkali. Use NSD only, never alkaline detergent. No enzyme treatment — protease will digest the fiber. No chlorine bleach. Cold water only (≤30°C). Amyl acetate is safe for brief contact but don't overwork.

spottingProtocol.agent: Scrape and blot — remove surface excess
spottingProtocol.equipment: Dull knife or credit card edge, clean white cloths or paper towels
spottingProtocol.instruction: Carefully lift any excess foundation from the surface using a dull knife edge or credit card — scrape in one direction, lifting away from the fabric. For liquid products (foundation, concealer), blot with a clean white cloth — press and lift, never wipe or rub. Rubbing cosmetics spreads the pigment over a larger area and drives the oil carrier deeper into silk. Place a clean towel behind the stain to catch any bleed-through. The goal is to remove as much surface material as possible before chemical treatment — every bit you remove mechanically is product you don't have to dissolve chemically.

spottingProtocol.agent: Solvent treatment — POG, rubbing alcohol, or micellar water
spottingProtocol.technique: Dissolve oil/wax carrier and soften film-former
spottingProtocol.equipment: POG or rubbing alcohol (70% isopropyl) or micellar water, clean white cloths, cotton swabs for precision
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place fabric face-down on a clean white towel. Apply your solvent to the BACK of the foundation stain — you're pushing the dissolved cosmetic OUT, not deeper in. POG is the professional choice for the oil/wax phase. Rubbing alcohol dissolves both oil and many cosmetic film-formers. Micellar water uses surfactant micelles to capture oil and pigment simultaneously. For foundation specifically, start with rubbing alcohol — it's effective on the widest range of cosmetic formulations. Apply to a cotton ball or cloth, dab onto the stain, and tamp gently. Watch the towel below for color transfer — that's pigment being extracted. Rotate towel positions and repeat until transfer diminishes.
spottingProtocol.suggestedProducts: Pyratex® POG, isopropyl alcohol 70%, Bioderma Sensibio Micellar Water

spottingProtocol.agent: Amyl acetate — for long-wear or waterproof formulas
spottingProtocol.equipment: Amyl acetate, clean white cloths, dauber
spottingProtocol.instruction: Long-wear and waterproof foundation formulas contain silicone polymers and cross-linked film-formers that resist standard solvents. If rubbing alcohol didn't fully lift the stain, amyl acetate (banana oil) provides stronger solvency for these polymer systems. Apply sparingly — amyl acetate is aggressive. Tamp and blot. You should see the remaining pigment release as the polymer matrix softens and breaks apart. For home users: a silicone-based makeup remover (like the ones designed for waterproof mascara) works on the same principle — it dissolves the silicone polymer film that protects the pigment.

spottingProtocol.agent: Surfactant emulsification — NSD or Dawn dish soap
spottingProtocol.technique: Pigment particle suspension and residual oil removal
spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD or Dawn dish soap, warm water, clean cloths, soft brush or old toothbrush
spottingProtocol.instruction: Mix 1 teaspoon NSD or Dawn in a cup of cool water (≤30°C). Apply to the foundation area and work gently with a soft brush. The surfactant serves multiple purposes: emulsifying residual oil, suspending freed pigment particles, and removing solvent residue. Cosmetic pigments (iron oxides, titanium dioxide, carbon black) are inorganic particles that need physical agitation plus surfactant suspension to flush away — they're too heavy to dissolve in any solvent. Work the area for 3-5 minutes with gentle brush strokes. Blot and check your progress. The stain should be significantly lighter.
spottingProtocol.suggestedProducts: Mulsolite® NSD, Dawn Ultra Dish Soap

spottingProtocol.agent: Oxidizer treatment — hydrogen peroxide 3% (for persistent dye ghost)
spottingProtocol.equipment: Hydrogen peroxide 3% or OxiClean solution, clean cloths
spottingProtocol.instruction: If a colored shadow persists after solvent and surfactant treatment, the cosmetic DYE (as distinct from the pigment) has bonded to the silk fiber. Cosmetic dyes — particularly D&C reds and iron oxide staining — require oxidative treatment to break the chromophore. Apply hydrogen peroxide 3% directly to the discolored area and monitor. The oxygen radical breaks the conjugated double-bond structure that creates visible color. For whites, you can use a slightly stronger approach — OxiClean mixed per label directions. For colored fabrics, TEST colorfastness in a hidden area first. Monitor throughout — don't leave oxidizer unattended on fabric. Use very dilute concentration on silk and monitor closely — test colorfastness first.

Flush thoroughly with cold water — multiple passes with clean cloths until no color, odor, or residue transfers. Compress between dry towels to extract moisture. Air dry in a well-ventilated area. For machine-washable items, follow with a cold-water wash cycle with regular detergent for complete rinsing. Inspect after drying under bright light — foundation residue can hide as a subtle discoloration that's only visible when the fabric is fully dry. If any trace remains, repeat from Step 2. Never machine dry until the stain is confirmed gone.

After the fabric is completely dry, perform a final inspection under bright light at multiple angles. Cosmetic stains — especially foundation — can leave a faint shadow or texture change that's invisible on wet fabric. Tilt the fabric under light and look for any discoloration, especially a subtle pinkish, yellowish, or brownish tinge. Touch the area — it should feel identical to surrounding fabric with no waxy, stiff, or greasy residue. If the stain passes both tests, you're done. If any trace remains, one more round of solvent + surfactant + oxidizer usually finishes it.

homeSolutions: Dawn dish soap (blue): Apply a drop directly to the dry foundation stain on silk. Work in gently, let sit 5 minutes, then blot with a damp cloth. Dawn's surfactants break down oil and grease. Repeat 2-3 times for set-in stains.

Rubbing alcohol (70% isopropyl): Dampen a clean white cloth and dab the foundation stain. Alcohol dissolves many oil-based compounds. Follow with soap and water. Best for fresh stains. Test colorfastness first on colored silk.

Baking soda absorption: For fresh foundation spills, cover immediately with baking soda. Let sit 15-30 minutes to absorb surface oil. Brush off and follow with dish soap treatment. Won't help with aged stains but excellent as a first response.

Cornstarch pre-treatment: Sprinkle cornstarch on the foundation stain, let absorb for 10-15 minutes. Brush off. Then apply dish soap solution. The powder-then-soap sequence handles more oil than either method alone.

Carbona Stain Devils (match to stain type): Choose the Carbona formula that matches your stain — #5 for fat/oil, #3 for ink/crayon, #1 for adhesives. Apply per label to the foundation stain. These consumer solvents are more effective than dish soap for set-in stains.
materialWarnings: NEVER use protease enzymes on silk — silk IS protein, and protease enzymes will digest the fiber itself, creating irreversible holes and weakening.
NEVER use chlorine bleach on silk — sodium hypochlorite dissolves protein fibers, causing yellowing and disintegration within minutes of contact.
NEVER use alkaline detergents (pH >8) on silk — alkali breaks peptide bonds in the fibroin chain, causing fiber degradation, loss of sheen, and permanent stiffness.
NEVER use acetone on silk — it can bleach colors and damage fiber structure. Use amyl acetate instead for brief solvent contact.
Cold water only (≤30°C / 86°F). Hot water denatures silk protein and can cause permanent shrinkage and texture change.
Always test dyes first — silk dyes bleed easily. Never wring silk. Never tumble dry. Iron on low heat with a press cloth between iron and fabric.
commonMistakes: Adding water first — water and oil don't mix. Applying water to a fresh foundation stain on silk drives the oil deeper into the fiber structure through displacement. Always start dry-side with a solvent or absorbent powder.
Rubbing instead of tamping — aggressive rubbing spreads foundation laterally and pushes it deeper into silk. Use gentle tamping or blotting motions that lift oil UP and OUT, not across and down.

Using heat before the stain is completely removed — putting a foundation-stained item in a hot dryer or pressing with an iron polymerizes any residual oil, converting it from a removable liquid to a permanently bonded plastic film. Air dry only until you've confirmed the stain is gone.

Using enzyme cleaner or hot water on silk — silk is a protein fiber. Protease enzymes digest protein = digest your fabric. Hot water denatures the protein and can cause permanent shrinkage and texture change. Cool water and NSD only.
scienceNote: Foundation's iron oxide pigments (CI 77491 red, CI 77492 yellow, CI 77499 black) are synthetic versions of natural mineral pigments. They're crystalline particles 0.1-1 micrometer in diameter with strong surface charges that make them cling to fabric fibers electrostatically. Titanium dioxide (CI 77891) is even more persistent — it's the whitening agent in paint, and its high refractive index means even a thin residual film is visible. This is why foundation stains sometimes leave a 'shadow' even after the color is gone — the TiO2 particles scatter light differently than clean fabric.
customerExplanation: Foundation makeup contains oil, pigment, and film-forming polymers. We dissolved the carrier, suspended the pigment particles, and flushed everything clean. Silk returned to its original appearance.
escalation: If the foundation stain persists after two full treatment cycles, professional dry cleaning is strongly recommended. Silk is a delicate protein fiber that limits available treatment options — a professional cleaner has access to controlled solvent immersion, steam systems, and fiber-safe chemistry that home treatment cannot replicate. Explain what you've already tried so they can adjust their approach.
callPro: Call a professional when: the garment is silk — delicate fibers have narrow treatment windows and professional equipment minimizes risk; you've completed two full treatment cycles and significant foundation residue remains — repeated treatment without improvement means the stain needs different chemistry or equipment; you see color bleeding, fiber damage, or unexpected texture changes during treatment — stop immediately and let a professional assess before the damage worsens.
solventNote: POG agents work across all solvent systems. For PERC shops: flush with Persec® between steps instead of water. For HC shops: flush with HC Boost™. For wet clean: water flush as written.
